title: Calibrating Machine Learning Classifiers in Python

yaml.title: Calibrating Machine Learning Classifiers in Python
yaml.author: Max Scheijen

markdown.markdown:
Machine learning classifiers are widely used in many different fields, from credit risk prediction to spam filtering. However, one important aspect that is often overlooked is the calibration of these classifiers. Calibration refers to the process of adjusting the predicted probabilities output by the classifier to more closely match the true probabilities of the events being predicted. In this blog post, we will explore why calibration is important and how it can be performed in Python using the CalibratedClassifierCV module from the scikit-learn library. We will also show how to visualize the results of the calibration process using several plots.


Probability estimates produced by machine learning classifiers are often overconfident, meaning that the predicted probabilities are too high or too low compared to the actual probabilities. This can lead to poor performance on test data and can result in incorrect decisions being made. For example, consider a credit risk prediction model that is trained to predict the probability that a borrower will default on a loan. If the model is overconfident, it may assign a high probability to a borrower who is actually at low risk of defaulting, leading the lender to reject the loan application even though it would have been a good decision to grant the loan.

Calibration can help improve the accuracy of the probability estimates produced by the classifier, leading to better performance on test data. In addition, calibrated classifiers can provide more reliable and interpretable probability estimates, which can be important in applications where the predicted probabilities are used to make decisions.

## Calibrating Classifiers in Python

The CalibratedClassifierCV module from scikit-learn provides a simple and efficient way to calibrate the probabilities of a classifier. It uses a method called "cross-validated calibration," which involves fitting the classifier on the training data and then calibrating the predicted probabilities on a held-out validation set using isotonic regression. Isotonic regression is a technique for fitting a monotonic function to the data that preserve the order of the data points.
